result:
The View from My Window at 3:17 A.M.

It is 3:17 a.m. and the city is a low-frequency hum, the kind that settles into your bones and vibrates like an old refrigerator. I am in my apartment on the seventh floor, the one with the crooked venetian blinds that I never quite fix because, in some quiet way, their off-kilter slant reminds me that perfection is not the goal of living. Outside, a single streetlamp flickers in Morse code no one ever taught me to read. I watch it for a long time, long enough that the interval between pulses begins to feel like a heartbeat. I wonder if the lamp is lonely, or if I am simply projecting, because humans are good at turning inanimate objects into mirrors and then complaining about the reflection.

I could tell you about the history of this building—how it was a shirtwaist factory in 1912 where women stitched cuffs until their fingertips bled, how the freight elevator still smells faintly of machine oil and burnt cotton—but tonight that feels like a story I borrowed from a museum. Instead I want to speak in the currency of now: the hush that occurs when the traffic lights switch to blinking amber because there aren’t enough cars to justify red and green, the damp hush of April that smells like rubber and lilac, the hush inside my rib cage where something unnamed keeps knocking.

Have you ever noticed that insomnia is a passport? The moment everyone else abdicates consciousness, borders dissolve. Time zones collapse. I scroll on my phone and a baker in Wellington posts a photo of custard tarts cooling on a rack, glossy and sun-bronzed, while here the moon looks like a nail clipping. I could comment “yum” with three heart emojis, and for an instant the planet contracts to the width of a flatbread. Then I look up and the physical room reasserts itself: the cereal bowl with the hairline crack, the stack of unopened mail wearing a delicate sweater of dust, the laptop cursor blinking like it’s impatient for me to finish the sentence I haven’t started.

Some people keep journals; I keep horizons. When I can’t sleep I stand at the window and collect them: the ridge of rooftops scribbled against the sky, the far away wink of the airport beacon cycling green-white-green, the low moon sliding between cell-tower ribs. If I watch long enough the city becomes a topographical map of my own synapses, lit and unlit, highway loops that spill into cul-de-sacs of forgetting. I think: every light out there is someone awake or afraid or in love, or all three at once. I think: if I could only triangulate their coordinates I would finally understand the theorem that stitches solitary nights into a quilt large enough to cover us all.

But maybe you don’t want geography. Maybe you want the inside of someone else’s skin. Fair enough. Step in. The foyer smells of cardamom because yesterday I spilled curry powder on the radiator and the grains slipped through the vent like sneaky pilgrims. The living room is an island of mismatched rugs: one from Oaxaca with red diamond eyes, one from an IKEA in New Jersey, one from a sidewalk on trash day that turned out to be a 1930s Deco design worth more than my car. (I don’t own a car. Figuratively speaking.) The couch hosts three pillows embroidered by my grandmother who believed that cross-stitch could keep dementia at bay; she died with a thimble on her thumb and every pillow says HOME IS WHERE STITCH MARKS ARE. I never corrected her grammar. I never told her that home is mostly a place where the Wi-Fi connects automatically. She would have laughed until she coughed, then offered me a butterscotch.

I talk to her lately. Not in a séance, candles-and-Ouija way; more like I narrate my life to the space she left, the way you keep texting a phone number long after the person has changed theirs. I tell her about the man on the subway who balanced a whole potted orchid on his palm during rush hour, how every petal survived the lurch from Union Square to Fulton. I tell her I finally learned to make her molasses cookies but substituted rye flour because that was all I had, and the cookies emerged tasting like autumn and apology. I tell her that the world feels like a Rubik’s cube soaked in water; the colors swell and no longer click. I can’t tell if she answers or if memory just echoes in the language I gave it.

At 3:46 a.m. a siren begins somewhere west, a rising wail that makes dogs converse across the alley. I imagine an EMT compressing a stranger’s chest, counting compressions in groups of thirty, humming “Stayin’ Alive” under his breath because the Bee Gees keep better time than any metronome. I once read that if you do CPR correctly you will break ribs, that the sound is like snapping fresh celery. I have never broken ribs, never had to, but the knowledge sits in a corner of my mind like an unused hammer. I send that anonymous stranger my version of prayer: May the ribs bend and not break. May the heart leap back to its saddle. May the siren fade into ordinary morning.

Ordinary morning is still three hours away. Between now and then the city and I will negotiate a series of small treaties. The radiator will clank out Morse that spells I AM TRYING. The upstairs neighbor— the one who practices tap at 2 a.m.— will finish her routine with a shuffle-ball-change that lands like a period at the end of a very long sentence. A garbage truck will chew on something metallic, tasting the bones of last night’s dinner. I will brew coffee at 4:15 because time is a rubber band and I am already stretched thin. The steam will fog the window and for a moment the skyline will disappear, as if the city itself is a secret I almost remembered.

I think about secrets. Everyone drags a few around like tin cans tied to a newlywed car. Some people try to bury them in backyard soil only to find they grow into indestructible trees. Some swallow them and spend years coughing up splinters. Mine live in the freezer next to the vodka bottle, written on post-its I never reread but also never throw away. They are small as lentils: the lie I told in seventh grade about who gave me the scar on my knee, the email I never answered from the friend who needed 500 dollars, the night I pretended to be asleep while my roommate cried on the fire escape. They keep well in subzero temperatures; they do not rot, they only shrink.

Tonight feels like the right night to thaw one. I pick the scar story. I said a black lab knocked me off my bike, but actually I slipped climbing the fence of the community pool because I wanted to feel water at night against skin, how the world might cradle me if I broke its rules. I needed stitches; the nurse asked if I felt safe at home; I nodded because safety seemed like a bigger country than the one I could afford. I never confessed to the pool trespass, never paid the fine. The scar is pale now, a tiny comma that suggests a pause rather than a stop. I run my thumb across it and apologize, not to anyone in particular, maybe to the chlorine-scented version of myself who believed trespass was synonym for freedom.

4:03. The first bird clears its throat. It is a house sparrow, unimaginative but reliable, the friend who shows up early to every party and still acts surprised by the cake. I open the window a crack and cold slips in like it has been waiting in line. Somewhere on the river a barge moans, low and hollow, the sound a dinosaur might make if compressed into a brass horn. I picture the captain in a wool cap, steam curling from his travel mug, guiding eight thousand tons of scrap metal through dark like someone knitting with steel. What does he tell himself at this hour? Maybe that every ounce once belonged to something useful—a tricycle, a filing cabinet, a merry-go-round horse—and usefulness outlives form. Maybe he hums Gordon Lightfoot, or maybe he listens to the hush between bells and thinks nothing at all.

I used to fear nothing-at-all. I grew up in a house where silence meant someone was angry; quiet was always the prologue to shouting. So I filled every room with talk radio, playlists, the dishwasher’s obedient churn. Then one day the amp on my stereo died and I had to sit with the sound of my own blood. Turns out it sounds like waves on the hull of a very small boat. Turns out I can survive that ocean. Since then I have tried to practice deliberate silence the way people practice yoga: awkwardly, frequently falling over, but always returning to pose. Tonight counts as a session. My legs ache from standing, but I stay. I breathe. I listen for the edge where city ends and cosmos begins.

At 4:22 a.m. the ISS passes overhead. I know this because I have an app that sends alerts, the same way other people get reminders about birthdays. It moves faster than any star, a white bead sliding on an invisible wire. I wave, though I’m never sure if the timing matches any of the astronauts’ windows. I like to imagine them looking down, seeing city lights as scattered seeds of possible life. Do they feel tenderness? Or is Earth like a glowing coin they’ll cash in when the mission ends? Once I read that long-duration astronauts sometimes struggle to sleep because without gravity their arms drift above their heads like surrendered ghosts. The brain, confused, wakes them in panic. I think: we are all, always, surrendering to unseen forces—call it orbit, call it rent, call it love.

When I was eight I wanted to be an astronaut until I learned space smells like seared steak and hot metal. That scent is the result of high-energy particles in low orbit reacting with trace Atomic oxygen; it clings to suits when crews return. Even now I’m unsure why the thought unsettled me. Maybe because I wanted heaven to smell like lilac or library. Maybe because I already knew I would never be brave enough to strap myself onto 3.6 million pounds of volatile propellant. Instead I became a cartographer of tiny distances: the space between cracked sidewalk slabs, the space between one heartbreak and the next, between an unmailed letter and the apology it contains. These are dangerous too, just quieter.

4:38. My phone battery dips below ten percent, red bar pulsing like a warning buoy. I obey its caution and flip to airplane mode. The room dims when the screen goes black, and for a moment I feel the way I did as a child at the beach when I walked too far from shore, toes just brushing sand while salt lifted me. The boundary is hard to locate until it’s gone. I think about how connectivity is supposed to erase loneliness yet somehow manufactures it. We are given infinite windows into rooms we cannot enter, kitchens at dawn where someone else’s kettle sings. I have watched strangers toast anniversary bagels and thought I miss you to people I never met. Tonight I am determined to miss only what is actually mine.

I close my eyes and inventory: the cat who ran away when I moved apartments, the friend who quit drinking and then quit our friendship, the hoodie stolen at a laundromat that smelled like cedar and the person who wore it. Each loss hangs in the wardrobe of memory, empty hangers tangled. I decide to keep one and let the rest swing free. I choose the cat. His name was Murasaki because his fur was the shade of purple dusk. He used to wait on the sill for pigeons, tail-tip twitching, eyes dilated into galaxies. When he vanished I papered the lobby with flyers that said “Please return my constellation.” No one did. Still, on nights like this I sense him prowling the seventh-floor hallway, impossible but welcome, another small country of belief.

4:52. Sky blushes periwinkle, a shy dawn I’ve seen too many times. The building’s boiler sighs off, concluding its graveyard shift. Somewhere a radio blares the morning farm report—soy futures up, hog prices down— though we are a twenty-minute subway ride from anything resembling a barn. The upstairs neighbor’s alarm goes off: mariachi horns. Her footfalls cross the ceiling like code. I translate: shower, kettle, toothbrush, exit. I imagine her descending the six flights to street level, emerging into half-light, breathing steam like a dragon wearing a peacoat. Does she know she is part of my 3 a.m. constellation? Probably not. We are all each other’s background extras, blurred until the camera decides otherwise.

I feel sleep finally pulling at my sleeve, polite but persistent, like a child who wants to show you something amazing: a marble that looks like Jupiter, a four-leaf clover the size of a freckle. I negotiate: five more minutes. Part of me wants to stay awake until the first commuter train goes by, because the Doppler effect always sounds like the city exhaling. Part of me wants to witness the bakery lights on the corner, see flour dust bloom under fluorescents. But my eyelids are heavy as wet wool. I shuffle to bed, pass the unopened mail, the bowl with the crack, the stack of half-read novels all insisting they contain answers. I promise them nothing.

Under the covers I lie on my back because I read that astronauts sleep strapped to walls; this is the closest I can get. The ceiling has a stain shaped like the Iberian Peninsula. I trace it with eyes half closed, reciting the names of provinces like sheep: Galicia, Asturias, Castilla y León. Somewhere in those syllables the world reduces to cadence, and cadence becomes dream. I hear the streetlamp outside click off, duty done. I hear the sparrow call again, louder now that darkness is losing its majority. I think: the planet just turned a fraction of a degree, and I with it, a tiny figure on a crust of basalt and gratitude. Sleep takes me the way a current takes a twig— without comment, without complaint.

When I wake, sunlight will stripe the floorboards, dust motes spinning like slow-motion carnival rides. People on the sidewalk will walk dogs that pause to read the news in hydrants. Someone will honk, someone will laugh, someone will push through the bakery door and inhale butter and certainty. None of them will know I borrowed the night for reconnaissance, that I crept to the edge of the observable universe and returned with nothing more than the same old heart, newly cracked open like a pistachio. That is fine. The world keeps its own ledger. And tonight—because there will be another tonight—the lamp will flicker again, the ISS will arc overhead again, the radiator will speak in its ancient dialect of warmth against all odds. I will stand at the window; I will wave; I will translate the hush into whatever language the day requires, a small citizen of the vast republic of almost dawn.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
